Entity_Description: Harrison Hot Springs is a resort village of 1,796 people (2021 Census) at the southern end of Harrison Lake in British Columbia's Fraser Valley, about 97 km east of Vancouver. Named for the natural mineral hot springs that feed its famous lakeside resort and public pool, the village has been a spa and tourism destination since the 1880s. Its compact core of hotels, motels, and cottages lines a sandy lagoon beach beneath the Coast Mountains, with Sasquatch Provincial Park just to the north and the farming communities of Agassiz and Kent minutes to the south.

Long_Description:
Harrison Hot Springs is unlike anywhere else in the Fraser Valley. While Abbotsford and Chilliwack grew into suburban cities on the Fraser River floodplain, this village of 1,796 stayed what it has been since the 1880s: a spa town at the south end of Harrison Lake, one of the largest lakes in southwestern British Columbia. The natural mineral hot springs that gave the village its name still feed the landmark Harrison Hot Springs Resort and the public pool, and the sandy lagoon beach along Esplanade Avenue draws crowds all summer, with Sasquatch Provincial Park and its forested lakes just up Rockwell Drive.

That identity shapes what {SERVICE} looks like here. The building stock is a distinctive mix: lakefront hotels and motels, seasonal cottages, condo and townhome developments built for retirees and weekenders, and modest year-round homes on the flat streets between the lagoon and the mountains. Hospitality properties cannot afford long shutdowns in peak season, and part-time owners need a {BUSINESS_TYPE} who can handle work while they are away. {COMPANY_NAME} understands both, backing every job with {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP}.

Geography adds its own demands. The village sits pinched between Harrison Lake and steep Coast Mountain slopes, where winter storms drop heavier rain than Metro Vancouver sees and outflow winds off the lake can bring freezes and snow. Properties near the lagoon and along the Miami River corridor deal with high groundwater and drainage concerns, a topic the whole valley takes seriously since the November 2021 atmospheric-river flooding. Buildings here face lake-facing wind exposure and moisture loads that a provider used to dry inland subdivisions simply will not anticipate.

The local economy runs on tourism, and events like the long-running Sasquatch Days keep the village busy beyond summer, but Harrison Hot Springs is also tied to its agricultural neighbours: Agassiz and the District of Kent are only 8 km down Highway 9, and Chilliwack is about 20 km away. {COMPANY_NAME} covers that whole corridor, so response to the village is measured in {RESPONSE_TIME}, not left to whenever a city crew can spare the drive.

From the resort strip on Esplanade Avenue to homes backing onto the mountainside, {COMPANY_NAME} delivers {SERVICE} matched to how this village actually lives and works. Request your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} or call {PHONE} to get started.
Local_Facts:
- Resort village at the southern end of Harrison Lake, one of the largest lakes in southwestern British Columbia
- Population: 1,796 (2021 Census), swelling far higher with summer tourism
- Named for natural mineral hot springs that have supported a spa and resort economy since the 1880s
- Home to the landmark Harrison Hot Springs Resort and a public hot springs pool
- Sasquatch Provincial Park lies just north of the village along Harrison Lake
- Located in the Fraser Valley Regional District, about 97 km east of Vancouver and 8 km from Agassiz
- Climate brings heavy winter rain off the Coast Mountains with warm summers and occasional outflow freezes
